Reefs\n\nOmar Valencia-Mendez 1, Andres Lopez-Perez 2, Betel Martinez-Guerrero 3,\nVirgilio Antonio-Perez 4 & Eduardo Ramirez-Chavez 5\n\n1 Programa de Doctorado en Ciencias Biológicas y de la Salud, Puerto Ángel, San Pedro Pochutla,\nOaxaca, 70902, México\n4 Buceo Huatulco, Ocotillo # 206, La Crucecita, Bahías de Huatulco, Oaxaca, 70989, México\n1 ovalenciam@outlook.com, 2 alopez@xanum.uam.mx (corresponding author),\n3 alpheusb@hotmail.com, 4 hoy.bucea@gmail.com, 5 eduardo@angel.umar.mx\n\nn\no\ni\nt\na\nc\ni\nn\nu\nm\nm\no\nC\nt\nr\no\nh\nS\n\nISSN 0974-7907 (Online)\nISSN 0974-7893 (Print)\n\nOPEN ACCESS\n\nAbstract: The Harlequin Shrimp Hymenocera picta is abundant in the\nIndo-Pacific and Central Pacific regions, but there are few reports of\nit from the eastern Pacific.  Two pairs of the Harlequin Shrimp were\nobserved  feeding  on  the  Sea  Star  Phataria  unifascialis  (Gray,  1840)\nin  the  reefs  of  Huatulco  National  Park,  Mexican  Pacific.    This  paper\nreports  the  occurrence  of  H.  picta  in  Mexican  Pacific  waters  and\nextends its previous distribution by 1,270km north of El Ocotal, Costa\nRica in the eastern Pacific equatorial zone.  In addition, we evaluate\nthe potential distribution of H. picta along the tropical eastern Pacific\nusing the Maximum-Entropy modelling algorithm.\n\nKeywords:  Caridea,  coral  reefs,  harlequin  shrimp,  Huatulco  National\nPark, sea star predator.\n\nHymenocera Latreille, 1819 is a monospecific genus\nthat contains Hymenocera picta Dana, 1852, commonly\nknown  as  “Harlequin  Shrimp”  or  “Painted  Shrimp”.\n\nHymenocera  is  a  small  decapod  crustacean  (~5cm\nin  TL)  belonging  to  the  superfamily  Palaemonoidea\nRafinesque, 1815 sensu De Grave & Fransen (2011).  Due\nto its amazing coloration, it is one of the most in-demand\ndecapod  crustacean  species  in  the  marine  ornamental\ntrade,  reaching  up  to  80  USD  per  pair  (Calado  et  al.\n2003).\n\nHymenocera picta was first described by Dana based\non  a  specimen  collected  in  Raraka,  Tuamotu,  French\nPolynesia.    Dana’s  figures  were  made  from  a  living\nspecimen;  however,  holotype  specimens  were  lost  in\nthe  wreck  of  the  “Peacock”  (Edmondson  1923).    This\nevent led to the complex and problematic taxonomy for\nthe species (Edmondson 1923).
